question: What is a key difference between a structure and a union in C?
options: A structure allocates memory for all its members simultaneously, while a union allocates memory for the largest member only.
A union allocates memory for all its members simultaneously, while a structure allocates memory for the largest member only.
There is no difference in memory allocation between structures and unions.
A structure can only hold one member at a time, while a union can hold all members.
explanation: A structure allocates separate memory for each of its members, so the total size is the sum of all members (plus padding). A union allocates a single shared memory block large enough to hold its largest member, allowing only one member to be active at a time.

options: 4 bytes (size of a typical int or float)
7 bytes (sum of int, char, and float)
The size is platform-dependent and determined by the compiler.
explanation: The size of a union is determined by its largest member. In most systems, both an int and a float are 4 bytes, while a char is 1 byte. Therefore, the union will be allocated 4 bytes of memory to accommodate the largest member.

question: Why would you use a union instead of a structure?
options: To create a variant record that can hold different types of data in the same memory location.
To combine different data types into a single, composite data type more efficiently.
To ensure all data members are initialized to zero by default.
To save memory when you need to access all members simultaneously.
explanation: Unions are primarily used for memory efficiency when you need to store different types of data in the same memory location but only one value is valid at any given time. This is ideal for implementing variant records or interpreting the same data in multiple ways.

options: Undefined/Unpredictable due to the overwrite of the shared memory.
A garbage value resulting from type punning.
explanation: Since a union uses a single shared memory location, the assignment 'var.y = 20.5' overwrites the bytes previously stored by 'var.x = 10'. The value of var.x is now corrupted, and accessing var.price would read the same bytes but interpret them as a float, resulting in an unpredictable value that is not 20.5 due to the way integers and floats are stored in memory.

question: How can you determine which member of a union was last written to (i.e., implement a tagged union) in C?
options: It is not possible; the programmer must manually track the active member using an external variable, often an enum.
By using the 'typeof' operator on the union variable.
The union data type has a built-in 'active_member' field for this purpose.
By checking the size of the union, which changes based on the active member.
explanation: Standard C unions do not have an inherent mechanism to track which member was last written to. The common practice is to create a tagged union (or discriminated union) by embedding the union within a structure that also contains a tag field (e.g., an enum) to explicitly track the currently active member.
